children.name: Myrmecophaga
children.description: The giant anteater — the largest of all anteaters and one of the most recognisable animals of South America. It can be 2.1 m long from nose to tail and weigh 35 kg. The giant anteater has no teeth; its stomach has a modified muscular lining that grinds insects like a gizzard. Its tongue is 60 cm long, covered in sticky saliva, and can flick in and out up to 150 times per minute. A single anteater may visit over 200 termite and ant nests per day, consuming up to 35,000 insects. It never destroys a nest completely — withdrawing before the insects mount an effective defence, allowing colonies to recover for future visits. Its massive, hooked forelaws are primarily used for tearing open nests and are so large that the animal walks on its knuckles.

children.name: Myrmecophaga tridactyla
children.description: Vulnerable. Found from Honduras to northern Argentina in savannah, grassland, and tropical dry forest. Road kill and habitat loss are the greatest threats. Can slash open a dog with its powerful foreclaws if cornered.

children.name: Tamandua
children.description: Tamanduas — medium-sized, semi-arboreal anteaters with a prehensile tail that functions as a fifth limb for gripping branches. Tamanduas are equally comfortable in trees and on the ground, raiding both arboreal termite nests and ground-level ant colonies. Like the giant anteater, they have a body temperature several degrees lower than most mammals (around 33°C) and lack teeth entirely. They produce a powerful, skunk-like musk from anal glands when threatened. Tamanduas are more widespread and common than the giant anteater, tolerated in disturbed habitats and secondary forest.

children.name: Tamandua tetradactyla
children.description: Widespread from Venezuela to northern Argentina. Often has a distinctive black vest pattern. Spends roughly equal time foraging on the ground and in trees. Roosts in hollow logs and tree cavities by day.

children.name: Tamandua mexicana
children.description: Found from Mexico to Peru and Venezuela. More arboreal than the southern species. Populations in Central America and South America differ in coat colouration. Uses the same nest sites repeatedly.

children.name: Cyclopes
children.description: The silky anteater — the world's smallest anteater, barely larger than a human fist at 17 cm body length and 175–400 grams. It is almost entirely arboreal and nocturnal, rarely if ever descending to the ground, and feeds almost exclusively on arboreal ant species. Camouflaged to resemble a seed pod of the silk-cotton tree (Ceiba), where it commonly shelters, the silky anteater can be astonishingly difficult to see even when pointed at it. When threatened, it adopts a boxing posture, raising its forelegs to protect its face. Largely unstudied due to its secretive, arboreal lifestyle.

children.name: Cyclopes didactylus
children.description: The smallest anteater, weighing less than 400 g. Wholly arboreal and rarely seen. Perfectly camouflaged against the seed pods of silk-cotton trees in which it shelters. Produces a single young which is carried on the mother's back.
